description: A psychology professor has collected Simon Task data from 25 students as a classroom demonstration. One additional participant (s99) was a grad student who accidentally left an autoresponder running, producing impossibly uniform reaction times. Using the data at ~/pebl/data/simon_task_data.csv (columns: participant, block, trial, condition, stimulus_side, correct, rt_ms), compute the Simon effect for each valid participant and generate group-level delta plots showing how the conflict effect changes across the reaction time distribution. Exclude participant s99 from the analysis. For the remaining 25 participants, analyze only correct trials (correct == 1). Calculate the individual Simon effect (mean RT incongruent - mean RT congruent). For the group delta plot, divide each participant's correct-trial RTs into five quintile bins (Q1-Q5). Within each quintile, calculate the Simon effect (mean RT incongruent - mean RT congruent), then average these quintile effects across all valid participants. Save the results to ~/pebl/analysis/simon_report.json. The JSON must contain a "participants" list with objects for each student (including id, mean_rt_congruent_ms, mean_rt_incongruent_ms, accuracy_congruent, accuracy_incongruent, and simon_effect_ms) and an entry for s99 (including id, excluded: true, and reason). It must also include "group_mean_simon_effect_ms" and a "group_delta_plot" object mapping quintiles Q1 through Q5 to their respective averaged Simon effect values.

metadata.data_source: Programmatically generated robust dataset exhibiting characteristic ex-Gaussian Simon Effect delta plots.
